catalyst.llm generation: alkaline Co(OH) 2 -decorated ultrathin 2D titanic acid nanosheet (H 2 Ti 6 O 13 )
catalyst.source: Here, we fabricate a novel alkaline Co(OH) 2 -decorated ultrathin 2D titanic acid nanosheet (H 2 Ti 6 O 13 ) catalyst, which rationally couples the structural and functional merits of ultrathin 2D supports with catalytically active Co species.
catalyst.context: The difficulty of adsorption and activation of CO 2 at the catalytic site and rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers severely restrict the CO 2 conversion efficiency.Here, we fabricate a novel alkaline Co(OH) 2 -decorated ultrathin 2D titanic acid nanosheet (H 2 Ti 6 O 13 ) catalyst, which rationally couples the structural and functional merits of ultrathin 2D supports with catalytically active Co species.Alkaline Co(OH) 2 beneficially binds and activates CO 2 molecules, while monolayer H 2 Ti 6 O 13 acts as an electron relay that bridges a photosensitizer with Co(OH) 2 catalytic sites.As such, photoexcited charges can be efficiently channeled from light absorbers to activated CO 2 molecules through the ultrathin hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite, thereby producing syngas (COH 2 mixture) from photoreduction of CO 2 .High evolution rates of 56.5 mol h -1 for CO and 59.3 mol h -1 for H 2 are achieved over optimal Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 by visible light illumination.In addition, the COH 2 ratio can be facilely tuned from 1:1 to 1:2.4 by changing the Co(OH) 2 content, thus presenting a feasible approach to controllably synthesize different H 2 CO mixtures for target applications.KEYWORDS: H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets, Co(OH) 2 decoration, photocatalysis, CO 2 reduction, syngas production

catalyst.source: High evolution rates of 56.5 mol h -1 for CO and 59.3 mol h -1 for H 2 are achieved over optimal Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 by visible light illumination.
catalyst.context: The difficulty of adsorption and activation of CO 2 at the catalytic site and rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers severely restrict the CO 2 conversion efficiency.Here, we fabricate a novel alkaline Co(OH) 2 -decorated ultrathin 2D titanic acid nanosheet (H 2 Ti 6 O 13 ) catalyst, which rationally couples the structural and functional merits of ultrathin 2D supports with catalytically active Co species.Alkaline Co(OH) 2 beneficially binds and activates CO 2 molecules, while monolayer H 2 Ti 6 O 13 acts as an electron relay that bridges a photosensitizer with Co(OH) 2 catalytic sites.As such, photoexcited charges can be efficiently channeled from light absorbers to activated CO 2 molecules through the ultrathin hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite, thereby producing syngas (COH 2 mixture) from photoreduction of CO 2 .High evolution rates of 56.5 mol h -1 for CO and 59.3 mol h -1 for H 2 are achieved over optimal Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 by visible light illumination.In addition, the COH 2 ratio can be facilely tuned from 1:1 to 1:2.4 by changing the Co(OH) 2 content, thus presenting a feasible approach to controllably synthesize different H 2 CO mixtures for target applications.KEYWORDS: H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets, Co(OH) 2 decoration, photocatalysis, CO 2 reduction, syngas production

catalyst.source: Ten milligrams of the prepared catalyst and 2 mg of Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 were added into a solution containing 450 L of acetonitrile (MeCN), 300 L of H 2 O, and 150 L of triethanolamine (TEOA).
catalyst.context: Preparation of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 Nanosheets.Layered H 2 Ti 6 O 13 was first synthesized via a calcination method. 34,35Then, 4 mL of tetrabutylammonium hydroxide was introduced into 1 L aqueous solution of the as-prepared layered H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (2 g) and stirred for a week.The dispersion was centrifuged to remove the precipitate.After that, 50 mL of HCl solution with a concentration of 1 molL was dropped into the obtained 50 mL colloid to obtain the precipitate.After washing with deionized (DI) water, the obtained H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets were vacuum-freeze-dried. Synthesis of Hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 Nanosheets.Briefly, 25, 50, 100, and 200 L of Co(NO 3 ) 2 solution (10 mgmL) were added into 50 mL of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheet colloids containing 5 mL of N 2 H 4 H 2 O, respectively.The mixed solutions were heated at 120 C for 12 h using 100 mL Teflon autoclaves.Then, 50 mL of KOH (1 M) was dropped into the cooled suspension, resulting in the precipitation of the hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite.After washing with DI water, the obtained products were vacuum-freezedried.Notably, to identify the hybrid composites, the obtained samples were denoted as x wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (x 0.3, 0.6, 1.0, and 1.8) in which x represented the mass percentage of Co(OH) 2 . Characterization.Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku MiniFlex 600 X-ray diffractometer) was utilized to characterize the crystal structure of the samples.In situ Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet Nexus 670) measurements were performed to explore the active species during the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction reaction.UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-vis DRS, Varian Cary 500) measurements were carried out to clarify the light absorption of the catalysts.X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI Quantum 2000) measurements were performed to investigate the composition and surface valence state of catalysts.Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Nova NANO-SEM 230 spectrophotometer), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL model JEM 2010 EX), and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanoscope Multimode IIIa, Veeco Instruments) were employed to analyze the morphology of the samples.The CO 2 adsorption was measured using an ASAP 2020 apparatus at 273 K (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA). Photoelectrochemical Measurements.The prepared samples (5 mg) were first ultrasonicated in 0.5 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide for 1 h.Then, 10 L of the mixed slurry was dropped onto a fluorinetin oxide (FTO) glass substrate with an area of 0.25 cm 2 .The periphery of the FTO glass is coated with epoxy resin for insulation and then dried under ambient conditions.The photocurrent of the samples was investigated by an electrochemical workstation (CHI650E) using a traditional three-electrode system.Specifically, a platinum plate was used as the counter electrode and a AgAgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode.The electrolyte is a sodium sulfate solution (0.2 molL).A 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 400 nm cutoff filter is selected as the light source. Photocatalytic Activity Test.The photocatalytic CO 2 reduction reaction was performed in a quartz reactor.Ten milligrams of the prepared catalyst and 2 mg of Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 were added into a solution containing 450 L of acetonitrile (MeCN), 300 L of H 2 O, and 150 L of triethanolamine (TEOA).The reactor was first vacuumed by a pump and then filled with high-purity CO 2 .After repeating the process three times, the reactor was irradiated with a 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 400 nm cutoff filter under mild stirring.The gas products were quantitatively analyzed using an Agilent 7890B gas chromatograph.Meanwhile, the liquid products were detected by liquid chromatography (Agilent 1290 Infinity II).directly prove the decoration of Co(OH) 2 on the surface of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 .

catalyst.llm generation: Co(OH) 2 -decorated ultrathin titanic acid nanosheet catalysts
catalyst.source: In summary, a series of alkaline Co(OH) 2 -decorated ultrathin titanic acid nanosheet catalysts have been fabricated by a soft chemical process and subsequently utilized for the syngas production from dye-sensitized CO 2 photoreduction.
catalyst.context: In summary, a series of alkaline Co(OH) 2 -decorated ultrathin titanic acid nanosheet catalysts have been fabricated by a soft chemical process and subsequently utilized for the syngas production from dye-sensitized CO 2 photoreduction.In the design, the H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets not only are a supporter of catalytically active Co(OH) 2 but also strongly bind the Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 photosensitizer to perform as an electron relay.The obtained Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite effectively promotes CO 2 adsorption-activation and facilitates photoinduced charge carrier separation and transfer.By regulating the modification amount of Co(OH) 2 , the composition of the produced syngas can be tuned flexibly in the photocatalytic process.As a result, the 0.6 wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 exhibits an optimal COH 2 yield rate of 56.559.3mol h -1 (ca.1:1) with good recyclability.The as-designed Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite could be recognized as a promising candidate for the future green photoconversion of CO 2 to solve environmental and energy problems.Moreover, this work proposes an enforceable approach to controllably synthesize different H 2 CO mixtures for industrial applications. s Supporting Information The Supporting Information is available free of charge at https:pubs.acs.orgdoi10.1021acsami.1c08251. Additional information as noted in the text, including XRD, SEM, and AFM images of the prepared catalysts (Figures S1-S5); photocatalytic activities of CO 2 reduction of the prepared catalysts (Figure S6); and BET EIS and UV-vis spectra of the prepared catalysts (Figures S7-S10) (PDF) Figure 1.Schematic synthesis of the Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 hybrid composite (a).XRD patterns of Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 with different weight amounts of Co(OH) 2 (b).SEM images of the as-prepared H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets (c) and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite (d). Figure 1a schematically illustrates the preparation of a Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 hybrid composite, which is realized by assembling Co(OH) 2 nanoparticles onto exfoliated H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets through a facile hydrothermal treatment.The ultrathin H 2 Ti 6 O 13 used in the synthesis is prepared by liquid exfoliation, as shown in Figures S1-S4.After decoration of Co(OH) 2 , XRD analysis is first performed to analyze the phase components of the Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composites with different weight contents of Co(OH) 2 .As presented in Figure 1b, all of the Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composites display similar XRD patterns to those of bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 , indicating the retention of the crystal structure of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 by the hydrothermal treatment.No characteristic peak of Co(OH) 2 is observed.This can be ascribed to the following factors: (i) the weight content of Co(OH) 2 is low and (ii) the loaded Co(OH) 2 is highly dispersed on the H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets. Figure 1c,d displays the SEM images of the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 and the hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 , which reveal the microstructure information of the samples.It can be seen that the prepared Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 presents a nanosheet morphology as that of bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 without obvious aggregation, indicating the preservation of the H 2 Ti 6 O 13 morphological characteristics after the hydrothermal process.The well-maintained ultrathin nanosheet is favorable to fulfill the catalytic function of the material.Figure 2a-c presents the TEM images of the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 and the hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 , which provide further microstructure and morphology information of the catalysts.Both of the samples display 2D structures with nearly transparent features, denoting the ultrathin nature (Figure 2a,b) of the nanosheets.Notably, in comparison with the smooth surface of the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 , some particles are observed on the nanosheet surface of Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 .High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis of the nanoparticles reveals lattice fringes of 0.23 nm (Figure 2c), which are well consistent with the (001) facets of Co(OH) 2 . 36Concurrently, clear lattice fringes of 0.32 nm are observed for the nanosheet substrate, which matches with the interplane spacing of the (130) plane of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 .Besides, the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental mapping of Co-(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (Figure 2d) discloses notable cobalt species distributed on the H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets.These findings Figure 2. TEM images of bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (a) and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (b).HRTEM image (c), EDX mapping (d), AFM image (e), and the corresponding height profile analysis along the green and blue lines (f) of Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 . Figure 3. High-resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p, O 1s, and Co 2p orbitals of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (a).UV-vis DRS spectra of the prepared samples (b). Figure 4. Gas production (a) over the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 and x wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composites (x 0.3, 0.6, 1.0, and 1.8).Recycled photoreduction of CO 2 under visible light irradiation for the optimal 0.6 wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 sample (b). Figure 5. Photoluminescence spectra (a), photocurrent responses (b), Nyquist plots (c), and CO 2 adsorption test (d) of the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheet composite.In situ FTIR spectra of CO 2 , H 2 O, and Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 interaction with Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 in the dark and under irradiation by visible light (e). Figure 6.Schematic diagram of the production of syngas from the photoreduction of CO 2 over the dye-sensitized Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite. , the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets show obvious H 2 evolution performance, yet with negligible CO 2 reduction activity.With the introduction of Co(OH) 2 , a significant CO yield is observed.The 0.3 wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 shows an obvious CO yield rate of 34.2 mol h -1 , accompanied by a moderate H 2 evolution rate of 57 mol h -1 .Such a finding indicates that Co(OH) 2 acts as a catalytically active site for the generation of CO.With the modification content of Co(OH) 2 reaching 0.6 wt , the hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 shows an optimal CO yield rate of 56.5 mol h -1 , N 2 adsorption-desorption measurements of the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composites reveal that the hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composites (22-25 m 2 g -1 ) show a higher Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area Photocatalytic CO 2 Reduction Performance under Different Reaction Conditions Ti 6 O 13 under different reaction conditions: a Ru-(bpy) 3 Cl 2 , 0.6 wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 , TEOAH 2 OMeCN solvent (volume ratio of 1:2:3), CO 2 atmosphere, and 400 nm.Without Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 .f Without light irradiation.g Reacted at 60 C.Without 0.6 wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 .i Co(OH) 2 TiO 2 . b N 2 atmosphere.c Without MeCN.d Without TEOA.e h 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composites.To further study the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction mechanism over Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 , in situ FTIR spectrum analysis is performed to simulate the reaction process.As shown in Figure 5e, the in situ FTIR spectra of pure Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 adsorbed with H 2 O-CO 2 before and after irradiation are recorded.All of the peaks marked with asterisks belong to Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 .With the addition of CO 2 and H 2 O, two major C-related active species are found at 1370 and 1263 cm -1 for Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 -Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 , which correspond to the characteristic vibration of the monocoordinated carbonates (m-CO 3 2-) and activated CO 2 molecule (CO 2 - Ti 6 O 13 and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheet composite.In situ FTIR spectra of CO 2 , H 2 O, and Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 interaction with Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 in the dark and under irradiation by visible light (e). https:doi.org10.1021acsami.1c08251ACS Appl.Mater.Interfaces XXXX, XXX, XXX-XXX B co-catalyst.context: The difficulty of adsorption and activation of CO 2 at the catalytic site and rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers severely restrict the CO 2 conversion efficiency.Here, we fabricate a novel alkaline Co(OH) 2 -decorated ultrathin 2D titanic acid nanosheet (H 2 Ti 6 O 13 ) catalyst, which rationally couples the structural and functional merits of ultrathin 2D supports with catalytically active Co species.Alkaline Co(OH) 2 beneficially binds and activates CO 2 molecules, while monolayer H 2 Ti 6 O 13 acts as an electron relay that bridges a photosensitizer with Co(OH) 2 catalytic sites.As such, photoexcited charges can be efficiently channeled from light absorbers to activated CO 2 molecules through the ultrathin hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite, thereby producing syngas (COH 2 mixture) from photoreduction of CO 2 .High evolution rates of 56.5 mol h -1 for CO and 59.3 mol h -1 for H 2 are achieved over optimal Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 by visible light illumination.In addition, the COH 2 ratio can be facilely tuned from 1:1 to 1:2.4 by changing the Co(OH) 2 content, thus presenting a feasible approach to controllably synthesize different H 2 CO mixtures for target applications.KEYWORDS: H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets, Co(OH) 2 decoration, photocatalysis, CO 2 reduction, syngas production

co-catalyst.context: In summary, a series of alkaline Co(OH) 2 -decorated ultrathin titanic acid nanosheet catalysts have been fabricated by a soft chemical process and subsequently utilized for the syngas production from dye-sensitized CO 2 photoreduction.In the design, the H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets not only are a supporter of catalytically active Co(OH) 2 but also strongly bind the Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 photosensitizer to perform as an electron relay.The obtained Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite effectively promotes CO 2 adsorption-activation and facilitates photoinduced charge carrier separation and transfer.By regulating the modification amount of Co(OH) 2 , the composition of the produced syngas can be tuned flexibly in the photocatalytic process.As a result, the 0.6 wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 exhibits an optimal COH 2 yield rate of 56.559.3mol h -1 (ca.1:1) with good recyclability.The as-designed Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite could be recognized as a promising candidate for the future green photoconversion of CO 2 to solve environmental and energy problems.Moreover, this work proposes an enforceable approach to controllably synthesize different H 2 CO mixtures for industrial applications. s Supporting Information The Supporting Information is available free of charge at https:pubs.acs.orgdoi10.1021acsami.1c08251. light source.source: High evolution rates of 56.5 mol h -1 for CO and 59.3 mol h -1 for H 2 are achieved over optimal Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 by visible light illumination.
light source.context: The difficulty of adsorption and activation of CO 2 at the catalytic site and rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers severely restrict the CO 2 conversion efficiency.Here, we fabricate a novel alkaline Co(OH) 2 -decorated ultrathin 2D titanic acid nanosheet (H 2 Ti 6 O 13 ) catalyst, which rationally couples the structural and functional merits of ultrathin 2D supports with catalytically active Co species.Alkaline Co(OH) 2 beneficially binds and activates CO 2 molecules, while monolayer H 2 Ti 6 O 13 acts as an electron relay that bridges a photosensitizer with Co(OH) 2 catalytic sites.As such, photoexcited charges can be efficiently channeled from light absorbers to activated CO 2 molecules through the ultrathin hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite, thereby producing syngas (COH 2 mixture) from photoreduction of CO 2 .High evolution rates of 56.5 mol h -1 for CO and 59.3 mol h -1 for H 2 are achieved over optimal Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 by visible light illumination.In addition, the COH 2 ratio can be facilely tuned from 1:1 to 1:2.4 by changing the Co(OH) 2 content, thus presenting a feasible approach to controllably synthesize different H 2 CO mixtures for target applications.KEYWORDS: H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets, Co(OH) 2 decoration, photocatalysis, CO 2 reduction, syngas production

reaction medium.source: Ten milligrams of the prepared catalyst and 2 mg of Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 were added into a solution containing 450 L of acetonitrile (MeCN), 300 L of H 2 O, and 150 L of triethanolamine (TEOA).
reaction medium.context: Preparation of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 Nanosheets.Layered H 2 Ti 6 O 13 was first synthesized via a calcination method. 34,35Then, 4 mL of tetrabutylammonium hydroxide was introduced into 1 L aqueous solution of the as-prepared layered H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (2 g) and stirred for a week.The dispersion was centrifuged to remove the precipitate.After that, 50 mL of HCl solution with a concentration of 1 molL was dropped into the obtained 50 mL colloid to obtain the precipitate.After washing with deionized (DI) water, the obtained H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets were vacuum-freeze-dried. Synthesis of Hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 Nanosheets.Briefly, 25, 50, 100, and 200 L of Co(NO 3 ) 2 solution (10 mgmL) were added into 50 mL of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheet colloids containing 5 mL of N 2 H 4 H 2 O, respectively.The mixed solutions were heated at 120 C for 12 h using 100 mL Teflon autoclaves.Then, 50 mL of KOH (1 M) was dropped into the cooled suspension, resulting in the precipitation of the hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite.After washing with DI water, the obtained products were vacuum-freezedried.Notably, to identify the hybrid composites, the obtained samples were denoted as x wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (x 0.3, 0.6, 1.0, and 1.8) in which x represented the mass percentage of Co(OH) 2 . Characterization.Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku MiniFlex 600 X-ray diffractometer) was utilized to characterize the crystal structure of the samples.In situ Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet Nexus 670) measurements were performed to explore the active species during the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction reaction.UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-vis DRS, Varian Cary 500) measurements were carried out to clarify the light absorption of the catalysts.X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI Quantum 2000) measurements were performed to investigate the composition and surface valence state of catalysts.Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Nova NANO-SEM 230 spectrophotometer), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL model JEM 2010 EX), and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanoscope Multimode IIIa, Veeco Instruments) were employed to analyze the morphology of the samples.The CO 2 adsorption was measured using an ASAP 2020 apparatus at 273 K (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA). Photoelectrochemical Measurements.The prepared samples (5 mg) were first ultrasonicated in 0.5 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide for 1 h.Then, 10 L of the mixed slurry was dropped onto a fluorinetin oxide (FTO) glass substrate with an area of 0.25 cm 2 .The periphery of the FTO glass is coated with epoxy resin for insulation and then dried under ambient conditions.The photocurrent of the samples was investigated by an electrochemical workstation (CHI650E) using a traditional three-electrode system.Specifically, a platinum plate was used as the counter electrode and a AgAgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode.The electrolyte is a sodium sulfate solution (0.2 molL).A 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 400 nm cutoff filter is selected as the light source. Photocatalytic Activity Test.The photocatalytic CO 2 reduction reaction was performed in a quartz reactor.Ten milligrams of the prepared catalyst and 2 mg of Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 were added into a solution containing 450 L of acetonitrile (MeCN), 300 L of H 2 O, and 150 L of triethanolamine (TEOA).The reactor was first vacuumed by a pump and then filled with high-purity CO 2 .After repeating the process three times, the reactor was irradiated with a 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 400 nm cutoff filter under mild stirring.The gas products were quantitatively analyzed using an Agilent 7890B gas chromatograph.Meanwhile, the liquid products were detected by liquid chromatography (Agilent 1290 Infinity II).directly prove the decoration of Co(OH) 2 on the surface of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 .

reaction medium.source: Ti 6 O 13 under different reaction conditions: a Ru-(bpy) 3 Cl 2 , 0.6 wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 , TEOAH 2 OMeCN solvent (volume ratio of 1:2:3), CO 2 atmosphere, and 400 nm.
reaction medium.context: In summary, a series of alkaline Co(OH) 2 -decorated ultrathin titanic acid nanosheet catalysts have been fabricated by a soft chemical process and subsequently utilized for the syngas production from dye-sensitized CO 2 photoreduction.In the design, the H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets not only are a supporter of catalytically active Co(OH) 2 but also strongly bind the Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 photosensitizer to perform as an electron relay.The obtained Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite effectively promotes CO 2 adsorption-activation and facilitates photoinduced charge carrier separation and transfer.By regulating the modification amount of Co(OH) 2 , the composition of the produced syngas can be tuned flexibly in the photocatalytic process.As a result, the 0.6 wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 exhibits an optimal COH 2 yield rate of 56.559.3mol h -1 (ca.1:1) with good recyclability.The as-designed Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite could be recognized as a promising candidate for the future green photoconversion of CO 2 to solve environmental and energy problems.Moreover, this work proposes an enforceable approach to controllably synthesize different H 2 CO mixtures for industrial applications. s Supporting Information The Supporting Information is available free of charge at https:pubs.acs.orgdoi10.1021acsami.1c08251. Additional information as noted in the text, including XRD, SEM, and AFM images of the prepared catalysts (Figures S1-S5); photocatalytic activities of CO 2 reduction of the prepared catalysts (Figure S6); and BET EIS and UV-vis spectra of the prepared catalysts (Figures S7-S10) (PDF) Figure 1.Schematic synthesis of the Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 hybrid composite (a).XRD patterns of Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 with different weight amounts of Co(OH) 2 (b).SEM images of the as-prepared H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets (c) and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite (d). Figure 1a schematically illustrates the preparation of a Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 hybrid composite, which is realized by assembling Co(OH) 2 nanoparticles onto exfoliated H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets through a facile hydrothermal treatment.The ultrathin H 2 Ti 6 O 13 used in the synthesis is prepared by liquid exfoliation, as shown in Figures S1-S4.After decoration of Co(OH) 2 , XRD analysis is first performed to analyze the phase components of the Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composites with different weight contents of Co(OH) 2 .As presented in Figure 1b, all of the Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composites display similar XRD patterns to those of bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 , indicating the retention of the crystal structure of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 by the hydrothermal treatment.No characteristic peak of Co(OH) 2 is observed.This can be ascribed to the following factors: (i) the weight content of Co(OH) 2 is low and (ii) the loaded Co(OH) 2 is highly dispersed on the H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets. Figure 1c,d displays the SEM images of the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 and the hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 , which reveal the microstructure information of the samples.It can be seen that the prepared Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 presents a nanosheet morphology as that of bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 without obvious aggregation, indicating the preservation of the H 2 Ti 6 O 13 morphological characteristics after the hydrothermal process.The well-maintained ultrathin nanosheet is favorable to fulfill the catalytic function of the material.Figure 2a-c presents the TEM images of the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 and the hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 , which provide further microstructure and morphology information of the catalysts.Both of the samples display 2D structures with nearly transparent features, denoting the ultrathin nature (Figure 2a,b) of the nanosheets.Notably, in comparison with the smooth surface of the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 , some particles are observed on the nanosheet surface of Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 .High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis of the nanoparticles reveals lattice fringes of 0.23 nm (Figure 2c), which are well consistent with the (001) facets of Co(OH) 2 . 36Concurrently, clear lattice fringes of 0.32 nm are observed for the nanosheet substrate, which matches with the interplane spacing of the (130) plane of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 .Besides, the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental mapping of Co-(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (Figure 2d) discloses notable cobalt species distributed on the H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets.These findings Figure 2. TEM images of bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (a) and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (b).HRTEM image (c), EDX mapping (d), AFM image (e), and the corresponding height profile analysis along the green and blue lines (f) of Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 . Figure 3. High-resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p, O 1s, and Co 2p orbitals of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (a).UV-vis DRS spectra of the prepared samples (b). Figure 4. Gas production (a) over the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 and x wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composites (x 0.3, 0.6, 1.0, and 1.8).Recycled photoreduction of CO 2 under visible light irradiation for the optimal 0.6 wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 sample (b). Figure 5. Photoluminescence spectra (a), photocurrent responses (b), Nyquist plots (c), and CO 2 adsorption test (d) of the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheet composite.In situ FTIR spectra of CO 2 , H 2 O, and Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 interaction with Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 in the dark and under irradiation by visible light (e). Figure 6.Schematic diagram of the production of syngas from the photoreduction of CO 2 over the dye-sensitized Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite. , the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets show obvious H 2 evolution performance, yet with negligible CO 2 reduction activity.With the introduction of Co(OH) 2 , a significant CO yield is observed.The 0.3 wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 shows an obvious CO yield rate of 34.2 mol h -1 , accompanied by a moderate H 2 evolution rate of 57 mol h -1 .Such a finding indicates that Co(OH) 2 acts as a catalytically active site for the generation of CO.With the modification content of Co(OH) 2 reaching 0.6 wt , the hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 shows an optimal CO yield rate of 56.5 mol h -1 , N 2 adsorption-desorption measurements of the bare H 2 Ti 6 O 13 and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composites reveal that the hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composites (22-25 m 2 g -1 ) show a higher Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area Photocatalytic CO 2 Reduction h 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composites.To further study the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction mechanism over Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 , in situ FTIR spectrum analysis is performed to simulate the reaction process.As shown in Figure 5e, the in situ FTIR spectra of pure Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 and Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 adsorbed with H 2 O-CO 2 before and after irradiation are recorded.All of the peaks marked with asterisks belong to Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 .With the addition of CO 2 and H 2 O, two major C-related active species are found at 1370 and 1263 cm -1 for Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 -Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 , which correspond to the operation mode.context: Preparation of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 Nanosheets.Layered H 2 Ti 6 O 13 was first synthesized via a calcination method. 34,35Then, 4 mL of tetrabutylammonium hydroxide was introduced into 1 L aqueous solution of the as-prepared layered H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (2 g) and stirred for a week.The dispersion was centrifuged to remove the precipitate.After that, 50 mL of HCl solution with a concentration of 1 molL was dropped into the obtained 50 mL colloid to obtain the precipitate.After washing with deionized (DI) water, the obtained H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheets were vacuum-freeze-dried. Synthesis of Hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 Nanosheets.Briefly, 25, 50, 100, and 200 L of Co(NO 3 ) 2 solution (10 mgmL) were added into 50 mL of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 nanosheet colloids containing 5 mL of N 2 H 4 H 2 O, respectively.The mixed solutions were heated at 120 C for 12 h using 100 mL Teflon autoclaves.Then, 50 mL of KOH (1 M) was dropped into the cooled suspension, resulting in the precipitation of the hybrid Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 composite.After washing with DI water, the obtained products were vacuum-freezedried.Notably, to identify the hybrid composites, the obtained samples were denoted as x wt Co(OH) 2 H 2 Ti 6 O 13 (x 0.3, 0.6, 1.0, and 1.8) in which x represented the mass percentage of Co(OH) 2 . Characterization.Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku MiniFlex 600 X-ray diffractometer) was utilized to characterize the crystal structure of the samples.In situ Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet Nexus 670) measurements were performed to explore the active species during the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction reaction.UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-vis DRS, Varian Cary 500) measurements were carried out to clarify the light absorption of the catalysts.X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI Quantum 2000) measurements were performed to investigate the composition and surface valence state of catalysts.Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Nova NANO-SEM 230 spectrophotometer), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL model JEM 2010 EX), and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanoscope Multimode IIIa, Veeco Instruments) were employed to analyze the morphology of the samples.The CO 2 adsorption was measured using an ASAP 2020 apparatus at 273 K (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA). Photoelectrochemical Measurements.The prepared samples (5 mg) were first ultrasonicated in 0.5 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide for 1 h.Then, 10 L of the mixed slurry was dropped onto a fluorinetin oxide (FTO) glass substrate with an area of 0.25 cm 2 .The periphery of the FTO glass is coated with epoxy resin for insulation and then dried under ambient conditions.The photocurrent of the samples was investigated by an electrochemical workstation (CHI650E) using a traditional three-electrode system.Specifically, a platinum plate was used as the counter electrode and a AgAgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode.The electrolyte is a sodium sulfate solution (0.2 molL).A 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 400 nm cutoff filter is selected as the light source. Photocatalytic Activity Test.The photocatalytic CO 2 reduction reaction was performed in a quartz reactor.Ten milligrams of the prepared catalyst and 2 mg of Ru(bpy) 3 Cl 2 were added into a solution containing 450 L of acetonitrile (MeCN), 300 L of H 2 O, and 150 L of triethanolamine (TEOA).The reactor was first vacuumed by a pump and then filled with high-purity CO 2 .After repeating the process three times, the reactor was irradiated with a 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 400 nm cutoff filter under mild stirring.The gas products were quantitatively analyzed using an Agilent 7890B gas chromatograph.Meanwhile, the liquid products were detected by liquid chromatography (Agilent 1290 Infinity II).directly prove the decoration of Co(OH) 2 on the surface of H 2 Ti 6 O 13 .
